skill.name: arm-cortex-expert
skill.description: Expert guidance for designing, implementing, and reviewing ARM Cortex-M firmware and peripheral drivers across Teensy, STM32, nRF52, and SAMD platforms.
skill.summary: Build reliable ARM Cortex-M firmware with safer drivers, interrupts, DMA, cache, and debugging patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: AI review found no malicious intent, prompt injection attempt, credential access, data exfiltration, or executable payload. The static findings are false positives caused by Markdown inline code, ARM/SAMD terminology, and embedded debugging guidance rather than dangerous runtime behavior.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Inline Code Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged locations are documentation examples and inline firmware API names, not Ruby or shell backtick execution. No evidence found of a command being run by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown guidance only, and the flagged backticks delimit prose examples. There is no script file, subprocess call, shell invocation, or user-controlled command construction.

low_findings.title: False Positive: SAMD Text Misclassified as Windows SAM Access
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged text refers to Microchip SAMD microcontrollers and related platform support. No evidence found of Windows SAM database access or credential harvesting.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each location uses SAMD as an embedded hardware family name. The surrounding context is microcontroller documentation, not Windows filesystem or account database access.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Firmware Terms Misclassified as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines discuss description text, maintainability, stale register reads, watchdog recovery, and driver design. No weak cryptographic algorithm is named or recommended.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The apparent matches come from ordinary words inside embedded firmware prose. There is no MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or cryptographic API usage in the inspected content.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Comment Text Misclassified as Deprecated Script Syntax
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged location is a Rust documentation comment describing safe critical-section access. It is not a JavaScript with statement or executable script content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 162 is inside a Markdown Rust code example and contains a comment. The skill package contains only SKILL.md, so there is no executable JavaScript scope behavior to exploit.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Debugging Guidance Misclassified as Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines describe valid MMIO address ranges and hardfault causes for embedded debugging. No evidence found of host reconnaissance or environment enumeration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The addresses and fault examples are ARM Cortex-M hardware concepts used for firmware validation. They do not query the host system, network, files, users, or secrets.

content.value_statement: Embedded firmware work fails when timing, interrupts, DMA, and memory ordering are handled casually. This skill gives practical ARM Cortex-M design and review guidance.
actual_capabilities: Designs firmware and driver structure for ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers.
Explains interrupt, NVIC priority, and critical-section patterns.
Reviews DMA buffer alignment and Cortex-M7 cache coherency choices.
Guides SPI, I2C, UART, ADC, PWM, USB, and BLE driver approaches.
Provides hardfault debugging steps and platform-specific constraints.
Compares C, C++, Rust, HAL, LL, and bare-metal implementation tradeoffs.
limitations: It does not access hardware, flash devices, or validate timing on a board.
It cannot replace vendor datasheets, errata, schematics, or safety certification.
The referenced implementation playbook is not included in the inspected package.
Generated firmware still needs target-specific compilation, review, and bench testing.

use_cases.title: Design a Peripheral Driver
use_cases.description: Plan a non-blocking driver with interrupts, buffers, platform APIs, and validation steps.

use_cases.title: Review Cortex-M7 DMA Safety
use_cases.description: Check buffer alignment, cache maintenance, memory barriers, and placement choices before hardware tests.

use_cases.title: Debug Faults and Timing Bugs
use_cases.description: Use structured hardfault, stack, watchdog, and priority analysis to narrow firmware defects.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Driver
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design an ARM Cortex-M driver for [peripheral] on [MCU]. Include init steps, API shape, interrupts, and validation checks.

prompt_templates.title: Review DMA Usage
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my DMA plan for [MCU family]. Check cache coherency, buffer alignment, memory placement, barriers, and failure modes.

prompt_templates.title: Debug a Hardfault
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through a hardfault on [Cortex-M core]. I have these register values and recent code changes: [details].

prompt_templates.title: Compare Architecture Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare bare-metal, HAL, LL, FreeRTOS, and Rust approaches for [product constraint]. Recommend a driver architecture and verification plan.

output_examples.input: Design a non-blocking SPI sensor driver for STM32H7.
output_examples.output: A structured driver plan with initialization, transaction states, chip-select timing, DMA considerations, interrupt flow, and bench validation steps.

output_examples.input: Review a Teensy 4.x audio DMA buffer strategy.
output_examples.output: A safety review covering 32-byte alignment, cache maintenance, memory region choices, ISR timing, and corruption risks.

output_examples.input: Explain why a Cortex-M firmware bug disappears when logging is enabled.
output_examples.output: A diagnosis path covering timing changes, missing memory barriers, interrupt priority issues, stale registers, and measurement methods.

best_practices: Provide the exact MCU, board, clock tree, peripheral, and toolchain before asking for implementation guidance.
Ask for validation steps that include compilation, bench checks, timing measurements, and failure handling.
Use the skill to review concurrency, DMA, cache, and interrupt assumptions before hardware tests.
anti_patterns: Do not request production firmware without sharing target hardware constraints and vendor API choices.
Do not copy generated register guidance without checking the reference manual and silicon errata.
Do not treat examples as certified safety logic for medical, automotive, or industrial control systems.

faq.question: Which microcontroller families does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It focuses on ARM Cortex-M platforms, including Teensy 4.x, STM32, nRF52, and SAMD devices.

faq.question: Can it generate complete driver code?
faq.answer: It can guide complete driver structure, but generated code still needs target compilation and hardware validation.

faq.question: Does it support Rust firmware work?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Rust embedded patterns, critical sections, atomics, and Cortex-M crate concepts.

faq.question: Is it useful for Cortex-M7 cache problems?
faq.answer: Yes. It specifically covers DMA alignment, cache maintenance, memory barriers, and non-cacheable memory choices.

faq.question: Can it debug hardware directly?
faq.answer: No. It cannot access probes or boards, but it can structure diagnostics from logs, registers, and symptoms.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: The audit found no malicious behavior. Static alerts were false positives from Markdown and embedded terminology.
